items.name: Hammer
items.text:
A hammer is one of the oldest tools humans ever made.
It has a heavy metal head and a long handle to hold.
You use it to hit nails into wood when building things.
There are many types. claw hammers, sledgehammers, and mallets.
A claw hammer can also pull nails out of wood!

items.name: Screwdriver
items.text:
A screwdriver is used to turn screws in and out.
It has a handle and a thin metal shaft with a different tip.
The two most common types are flat-head and Phillips (cross-shaped).
You use it to put together furniture, toys, and electronics.
Electricians use different screwdrivers that keep safe from electricity.

items.name: Ruler
items.text:
A ruler is a flat strip used to measure length and draw straight lines.
Most rulers are 30 cm (12 inches) long.
They can be made of wood, plastic, or metal.
Students use rulers every day in school for drawing and measuring.
Engineers and architects use very precise rulers for their designs.

items.name: Pliers
items.text:
It is a gripping tool used to hold, bend, and cut things.
They have two handles and two jaws that squeeze together.
Electricians use pliers to bend and cut wires safely.
There are many kinds. needle-nose, cutting, and locking pliers.
Needle-nose pliers can reach into very tight spaces.

items.name: Drill
items.text:
A drill is a tool that spins a sharp bit to make holes.
Electric drills are the most common. they are fast and powerful.
Drills are used to make holes in wood, metal, and walls.
You can also use a drill to drive screws quickly.
The bit is the sharp part at the front. it comes in many sizes.

items.name: Spirit Level
items.text:
A spirit level is a tool used to check if a surface is perfectly flat or straight.
Inside it is a sealed tube with liquid and a small air bubble.
When the bubble sits exactly between two lines. the surface is level!
Builders use spirit levels when laying floors and putting up walls.
Carpenter shelves must be perfectly level or things will slide off.

items.name: Chisel
items.text:
A chisel is a cutting tool with a sharp flat blade at the end.
You hit the top of it with a mallet to carve or cut.
Woodworkers use chisels to carve beautiful shapes into wood.
Stonemasons use different heavy chisels to shape rock and stone.
Chisels come in many widths. from tiny ones to very wide ones.

items.name: Wrench
items.text:
A wrench is a tool used to grip and turn nuts and bolts.
It is also known as a spanner in many countries including India.
There are open-end wrenches, box wrenches, and adjustable wrenches.
An adjustable wrench can be set to fit many different sizes.
Mechanics always keep a full set of wrenches in their toolbox.

items.name: Saw
items.text:
A saw is a cutting tool with a row of sharp teeth along its blade.
You move it back and forth to cut through wood, metal, or plastic.
The handsaw is the simplest. just a blade with a handle.
Circular saws are electric and spin the blade very fast.
Jigsaws can cut curved shapes. useful for making furniture.

items.name: Tape Measure
items.text:
A tape measure is a flexible ruler coiled inside a small case.
You pull the metal tape out to measure long distances.
Most tape measures are 3 to 10 metres long.
The end hook catches on the edge of what you are measuring.
Builders, carpenters, and tailors use tape measures every day.

items.name: Sandpaper
items.text:
It is a rough paper coated with tiny grains of abrasive material.
You rub it on surfaces to make them smooth.
The number on sandpaper tells you how rough it is. lower numbers are rougher.
Carpenters use coarse sandpaper first, then fine sandpaper for a smooth finish.
It is used on wood, metal, and even walls before painting.

items.name: Clamp
items.text:
A clamp is a device used to hold two things tightly together.
Carpenters use clamps to hold pieces of wood while the glue dries.
Without a clamp the glued parts would slide apart before they set.
There are many types. G-clamps, bar clamps, spring clamps, and more.
C-clamps look like the letter C with a screw that tightens the jaw.

items.name: Paint Brush
items.text:
A paint brush is used to apply paint, varnish, or glue to surfaces.
Brushes come in many sizes. small for detail work, large for big areas.
The bristles are the hairy part that holds and spreads the paint.
Natural bristles come from animals, synthetic ones are made from nylon.
Always clean your brush immediately after painting. dried paint ruins it.

items.name: Torque Wrench
items.text:
A torque wrench is a different tool used to tighten bolts and nuts to an exact force.
It makes sure the bolt is not too loose or too tight.
Mechanics use it when fixing cars, trucks, and motorbikes.
It clicks when you have tightened enough. so you know to stop!
Too much tightening can break the bolt, so this tool helps a lot.

items.name: Black Hole
items.text:
A black hole is a place in space where gravity is very strong.
Not even light can escape from it. that is why it looks black!
Black holes form when a very large star runs out of fuel and collapses.
The centre of a black hole is known as the singularity.
Around it is the event horizon. the point of no return.

items.name: Milky Way
items.text:
The Milky Way is the galaxy we live in.
It is a huge spiral shape with billions of stars.
On a clear night away from city lights, you can see it as a faint band across the sky.
Our Solar System is found on one of its spiral arms.
The Milky Way is about 100,000 light-years wide.

items.name: Nebula
items.text:
A nebula is a giant cloud of gas and dust floating in space.
The word nebula comes from Latin and means cloud.
Nebulae are the birthplaces of new stars!
When gravity pulls the gas together, it heats up and becomes a star.
They glow in beautiful colours. red, blue, purple, and green.

items.name: Comet
items.text:
A comet is a small icy body that moves through space.
When it gets close to the Sun, the ice melts and forms a glowing tail.
The tail always points away from the Sun because of solar wind.
Comets are made of ice, dust, rock, and frozen gases.
Halley's Comet is the most well known. it visits Earth every 75 to 76 years.

items.name: Saturn
items.text:
It is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest in our Solar System.
It is well known for its stunning rings made of ice and rock.
It is so light it would actually float on water!
It is a gas giant. there is no solid ground to stand on.
It has 146 known moons. more than any other planet.

items.name: Mars
items.text:
It is the fourth planet from the Sun and our closest neighbour.
It is known as the Red Planet because of its reddish iron-rich dust.
It has the tallest volcano in the Solar System. Olympus Mons.
It is three times taller than Mount Everest!
It also has a giant canyon known as Valles Marineris. longer than the whole of India.

items.name: The Moon
items.text:
It is Earth's only natural satellite and our closest neighbour in space.
It is about 384,000 kilometres away from Earth.
It has no air, no weather, and no sound.
Footprints left by Apollo astronauts in 1969 are still there. undisturbed!
The Moon's gravity causes the tides in our oceans every day.

items.name: Sun
items.text:
The Sun is the star at the centre of our Solar System.
It is a giant ball of hot gas. mostly hydrogen and helium.
The Sun is so large that about 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it.
Its surface temperature is about 5,500 degrees Celsius.
The core is even hotter. about 15 million degrees!

items.name: Jupiter
items.text:
It is the largest planet in our Solar System.
It is so big that all the other planets could fit inside it!
It is a gas giant. it has no solid surface to stand on.
The well known Great Red Spot is a storm that has been raging for over 350 years!
It has at least 95 known moons.

items.name: Northern Lights
items.text:
The Northern Lights, or Aurora Borealis, are one of nature's most stunning displays.
They appear as colourful ribbons of light dancing across the night sky.
They happen when charged particles from the Sun hit Earth's atmosphere.
The colours. green, red, blue, and purple. depend on which gas particles are hit.
Green is the most common colour and is caused by oxygen.

items.name: International Space Station
items.text:
The International Space Station, or ISS, is a large spacecraft orbiting Earth.
It flies about 400 kilometres above us. travelling at 28,000 km per hour.
At that speed it orbits Earth 16 times every single day!
The ISS is as big as a football field and has been continuously occupied since 2000.
Astronauts from many countries live and work there together.

items.name: Shooting Star
items.text:
A shooting star is not actually a star. it is a tiny piece of rock or dust from space.
When it enters Earth's atmosphere, it burns up due to friction with the air.
This burning creates the bright streak of light we see in the sky.
The proper name for a shooting star is a meteor.
If the rock survives and lands on Earth, it is known as a meteorite.

items.name: Supernova
items.text:
A supernova is a very big explosion that happens when a large star dies.
It is the most powerful explosion in the universe.
A supernova can briefly shine brighter than an entire galaxy of billions of stars!
When a star runs out of fuel, its core collapses in less than a second.
The outer layers are blasted away in a giant shockwave.

items.name: Asteroid Belt
items.text:
The Asteroid Belt is a region of space between Mars and Jupiter.
It contains millions of rocky and metallic objects known as asteroids.
Asteroids are leftovers from the formation of our Solar System 4.6 billion years ago.
The largest asteroid, Ceres, is so big it is known as a dwarf planet.
Despite having millions of asteroids, the belt is mostly empty space.

items.name: Airplane
items.text:
An airplane is a flying machine that carries people and goods through the air.
It has wings that help lift it up when it moves fast.
The first airplane was built by the Wright Brothers in 1903.
Modern planes can carry hundreds of passengers at once.
The engines push the plane forward. this is known as thrust.

items.name: Submarine
items.text:
A submarine is a different ship that can travel underwater.
It dives below the surface by filling tanks with water to become heavier.
To come back up, it pushes the water out with air.
Submarines are used by the navy to stay hidden in the ocean.
Scientists also use small submarines to explore the deep sea.

items.name: Helicopter
items.text:
A helicopter is an aircraft that can fly in any direction. even hover in one spot!
Instead of wings, it uses spinning blades on top known as rotors.
The rotors spin very fast and push air downward to lift the helicopter up.
Helicopters are used to rescue people from mountains and flooded areas.
Doctors use air ambulance helicopters to reach injured people quickly.

items.name: Bicycle
items.text:
A bicycle is a two-wheeled machine powered by pedalling with your feet.
When you push the pedals, a chain turns the back wheel.
Bicycles do not need petrol. they run entirely on human energy!
They are one of the most eco-friendly ways to travel.
The first bicycle was invented in Germany in the early 1800s.

items.name: Rocket
items.text:
A rocket is a machine that uses burning fuel to propel itself through space.
Unlike airplanes, rockets carry their own oxygen. so they work in space where there is no air.
The burning fuel creates hot gases that shoot out the back, pushing the rocket forward.
This is the same principle as a balloon releasing air!
The Saturn V rocket that took astronauts to the Moon is still the most powerful ever built.

items.name: Train
items.text:
A train is a machine that runs on steel rails and carries passengers or goods.
The first steam train was invented by George Stephenson in 1825.
Modern trains are powered by electricity or diesel engines.
The fastest trains in the world are Maglev trains. they float on magnets!
Japan's Shinkansen (bullet train) moves at over 300 km per hour.

items.name: Ship
items.text:
Ships are large water vessels that carry people and cargo across oceans.
They have been used for thousands of years for exploration and trade.
The Titanic was one of the most well known ships. it sank in 1912 after hitting an iceberg.
Modern container ships carry thousands of metal boxes of goods.
About 90% of everything we buy was transported by ship at some point!

items.name: Tractor
items.text:
A tractor is a powerful machine used mainly on farms.
It has huge rear wheels and a strong engine to pull heavy loads.
Farmers attach different tools. ploughs, harrows, and seeders. to the back.
A plough turns over the soil before planting crops.
Modern tractors have comfortable cabins with air conditioning and GPS!

items.name: Ambulance
items.text:
An ambulance is a different machine used to transport sick or injured people to hospital.
It carries medical equipment and trained paramedics inside.
Ambulances have flashing lights and loud sirens so other vehicles move out of the way.
Inside, paramedics can give first aid and emergency treatment on the way to hospital.
Some ambulances are boats. used to reach people injured on water.

items.name: Electric Car
items.text:
An electric car runs entirely on electricity stored in large batteries.
Instead of a petrol engine, it has an electric motor that is silent and smooth.
Electric cars produce no exhaust fumes. so they are much better for the environment.
They are cheaper to run because electricity costs less than petrol.
You charge them by plugging them into a charging point. just like a phone!

items.name: Hovercraft
items.text:
A hovercraft is a machine that moves on a cushion of air.
Powerful fans blow air underneath, lifting the craft off the surface.
Hovercrafts can travel over water, land, mud, ice, and even swamps!
This makes them very useful in places where boats and cars cannot go.
The military uses hovercrafts to land soldiers on beaches.

items.name: Cable Car
items.text:
A cable car is a machine that hangs from a steel cable and moves up steep hills or mountains.
The cable is powered by a motor at one end that pulls it along.
Cable cars are used where roads are too steep for normal vehicles.
San Francisco is well known for its historic cable cars that run on city streets.
In the mountains, cable cars carry skiers and tourists to great heights.

items.name: Fire Engine
items.text:
A fire engine is a large machine used by firefighters to put out fires.
It carries a huge tank of water and a powerful pump.
The long ladder on top can extend many floors high to reach trapped people.
Firefighters also use it to carry hoses, axes, and breathing equipment.
The loud siren and bright red colour warn people to clear the road.

items.name: Spacecraft
items.text:
A spacecraft is a machine designed to travel in outer space.
It can carry humans or fly without a crew. controlled from Earth.
The first spacecraft, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957.
The Apollo spacecraft carried astronauts to the Moon between 1969 and 1972.
India's Chandrayaan-3 successfully landed on the Moon's south pole in 2023. a historic achievement!

items.name: Cheetah
items.text:
The cheetah is the fastest land animal on Earth.
It can run at speeds up to 120 km per hour in short bursts.
Cheetahs have spotted fur that helps them hide in the grass.
They live mainly in Africa and parts of Iran.
Unlike other big cats, cheetahs cannot roar. they purr like house cats!

items.name: Elephant
items.text:
Elephants are the largest land animals on Earth.
They have long trunks used for breathing, smelling, drinking, and picking things up.
An elephant's trunk has over 40,000 muscles!
There are two kinds: African elephants and Asian elephants.
African elephants are larger and have bigger ears.

items.name: Dolphin
items.text:
Dolphins are highly smart marine mammals.
They are not fish. they breathe air through a blowhole on top of their head.
Dolphins live in oceans, seas, and some rivers around the world.
They use echolocation. sending out sound waves to find food and navigate.
Dolphins are very social and live in groups known as pods.

items.name: Penguin
items.text:
Penguins are birds that cannot fly but are excellent swimmers.
They use their flippers to glide gracefully through the water.
Most penguins live in the cold Southern Hemisphere, especially Antarctica.
Emperor Penguins are the tallest. they can grow up to 1.2 metres!
Penguins huddle together in huge groups to keep warm in freezing temperatures.

items.name: Tiger
items.text:
The tiger is the largest wild cat in the world.
Tigers are known for their beautiful orange fur with black stripes.
Every tiger has a unique stripe pattern. just like human fingerprints!
Tigers are excellent swimmers and love to cool off in rivers and lakes.
They are solitary hunters. they hunt alone, not in packs.

items.name: Eagle
items.text:
Eagles are large, powerful birds of prey with incredible eyesight.
They can spot a rabbit from over 3 kilometres away!
Eagles build huge nests known as eyries. often on cliff edges or tall trees.
The Bald Eagle is the national bird of the United States.
The eagle is associated with Lord Vishnu's machine, Garuda, in Hindu tradition.

items.name: Octopus
items.text:
The octopus is one of the most smart creatures in the ocean.
It has eight arms lined with powerful suckers for gripping.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood!
They can change their colour and texture in an instant to camouflage themselves.
When threatened, they shoot a cloud of black ink to confuse the predator.

items.name: Polar Bear
items.text:
Polar bears are the world's largest land predators.
They live in the Arctic. one of the coldest places on Earth.
Polar bears have thick white fur and a layer of fat to keep them warm.
Under their white fur, their skin is actually black. to absorb heat from the sun.
Polar bears are excellent swimmers. they can swim over 100 km without rest!

items.name: Whale
items.text:
Whales are the largest animals that have ever existed on Earth.
The Blue Whale can grow up to 30 metres long and weigh 200 tonnes!
Whales are mammals. they breathe air through a blowhole on top of their head.
They come up to the surface to breathe and then dive deep into the ocean.
Whales communicate with songs. humpback whale songs can travel hundreds of kilometres!

items.name: Gorilla
items.text:
Gorillas are the largest primates on Earth and our close relatives.
They live in the forests of central Africa.
Gorillas live in family groups led by a dominant male known as a silverback.
Silverbacks have a patch of silver-grey hair on their back. showing their maturity.
Gorillas are gentle giants. they are not aggressive unless threatened.

items.name: Honey Bee
items.text:
Honey bees are small but very important insects.
They pollinate flowers. helping plants reproduce and make fruit.
Without bees, most of our fruits, vegetables, and flowers would disappear!
A honey bee hive can contain up to 80,000 bees working together.
The queen bee lays up to 2,000 eggs per day.

items.name: Crocodile
items.text:
Crocodiles are among the most very old animals on Earth. they outlived the dinosaurs!
They have barely changed in 200 million years.
Crocodiles have the strongest bite of any animal in the world.
Despite this, their jaw muscles for opening are very weak!
They are cold-blooded. they need the sun to warm their body.

items.name: Butterfly
items.text:
Butterflies are beautiful insects known for their colourful wings.
They start life as an egg, hatch as a caterpillar, form a chrysalis, then emerge as a butterfly.
This amazing transformation is known as metamorphosis.
A butterfly's wings are covered in tiny scales that create their patterns and colours.
Butterflies taste with their feet. they can tell if a leaf is good to eat just by landing!

items.name: Giraffe
items.text:
The giraffe is the tallest animal on Earth. adults can reach 5 to 6 metres tall!
Their long necks help them reach leaves at the tops of trees.
Despite the long neck, giraffes have exactly the same number of neck bones as humans. just 7!
Each bone is simply much larger in a giraffe.
Giraffes have uniquely patterned spots. no two giraffes look exactly alike.

items.name: Banyan Tree
items.text:
The Banyan tree is one of the most amazing trees in the world.
It starts life as a seed that sprouts on another tree.
Over time, its roots hang down and reach the ground.
These aerial roots become thick trunks, making one tree look like a forest!
The Great Banyan Tree in Kolkata, India, is the widest tree in the world.

items.name: Lotus
items.text:
The lotus is a beautiful flower that grows in muddy water.
Even though it grows in mud, it stays perfectly clean and pure.
The lotus is the national flower of India and Egypt.
In Hinduism and Buddhism, the lotus is a symbol of purity and beauty.
Goddess Lakshmi and Lord Brahma are often shown sitting on a lotus.

items.name: Neem Tree
items.text:
The neem tree is one of the most useful trees in India and the world.
Almost every part of it. leaves, bark, seeds, and oil. has a use.
Neem leaves are used in traditional medicine to treat fever and skin problems.
Neem oil is a natural pesticide. it protects crops without harmful chemicals.
In many Indian villages, people use neem twigs as toothbrushes!

items.name: Bamboo
items.text:
It is one of the fastest-growing plants on Earth.
Some species can grow 90 cm in a single day. you can almost watch it grow!
It is actually a type of grass, not a tree.
It is very strong. stronger than many types of wood and even some steel.
It is used to build houses, bridges, furniture, and even bicycles.

items.name: Mango Tree
items.text:
The mango tree is one of the most beloved trees in India.
India is the largest producer of mangoes in the entire world.
The mango is the national fruit of India.
Mango trees can live for over 300 years and still bear fruit!
The tree gives nice shade in the hot summer months.

items.name: Tulsi
items.text:
Tulsi, or holy basil, is one of the most sacred plants in Hinduism.
Almost every Hindu home has a tulsi plant in the courtyard or on the balcony.
It is worshipped daily. especially by women who water and pray around it.
It is believed to purify the air and bring positive energy to the home.
In Ayurveda, tulsi is used to treat colds, coughs, and fevers.

items.name: Peepal Tree
items.text:
The Peepal tree is one of the most sacred trees in Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism.
Lord Buddha attained enlightenment while meditating under a Peepal tree.
This particular tree is known as the Bodhi tree and is revered worldwide.
In Hinduism, the Peepal is believed to be the home of Lord Vishnu.
The Peepal tree is unique. it releases oxygen even at night, unlike most plants.

items.name: Coconut Tree
items.text:
The coconut tree is known as the Tree of Life. every single part of it is useful.
The fruit gives coconut water, coconut milk, coconut oil, and coconut flesh.
Coconut oil is used for cooking, hair care, and skin care.
The hard shell of the coconut is used to make bowls, utensils, and crafts.
Coconut fibre (coir) is used to make ropes, mats, and brushes.

items.name: Sunflower
items.text:
Sunflowers are tall, bright yellow flowers that love sunlight.
They can grow up to 3 metres tall. taller than most adults!
Sunflowers turn their heads to follow the Sun as it moves across the sky.
This movement is known as heliotropism.
The centre of a sunflower is actually made up of hundreds of tiny flowers!

items.name: Aloe Vera
items.text:
It is a succulent plant known for its thick, fleshy leaves.
The clear gel inside the leaves is well known for healing skin burns, cuts, and rashes.
It gel is one of the most widely used natural remedies in the world.
It is also used in face creams, shampoos, and medicines.
It can live long dry periods because it stores water in its leaves.

items.name: Cactus
items.text:
A cactus is a plant that lives in hot, dry deserts.
Instead of flat leaves, it has sharp spines to keep safe itself from animals.
The thick stem stores water inside, like a water tank.
Some cacti can live for years without rain.
Cacti can grow very slowly. some take 10 years just to be 2 cm tall.

items.name: Rose
items.text:
The rose is one of the most beautiful and beloved flowers in the world.
Roses come in many colours. red, pink, white, yellow, and orange.
A red rose is a universal symbol of love and romance.
Roses have been cultivated for over 5,000 years. very old Romans grew them in huge gardens.
The rose is the national flower of England and the United States.

items.name: Jasmine
items.text:
It is a beautiful flowering plant known for its very sweet fragrance.
In India, jasmine flowers are known as Mogra or Chameli and are deeply loved.
Women wear jasmine garlands in their hair on festivals and different occasions.
It flowers are used to make jasmine tea. one of the most popular teas in China.
It oil is one of the most prized ingredients in the world's finest perfumes.

items.name: Venus Flytrap
items.text:
The Venus Flytrap is one of the most fascinating plants in the world. it eats insects!
It is a carnivorous plant. meaning it gets nutrients by trapping and digesting animals.
Its leaves form a pair of hinged lobes with tiny trigger hairs inside.
When an insect touches the trigger hairs twice, the trap snaps shut in less than a second!
The plant then slowly digests the insect over several days.

items.name: Rainbow
items.text:
A rainbow appears when sunlight passes through water droplets in the air.
The droplets act like tiny prisms and split light into its colours.
A rainbow always has seven colours: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.
You can remember them with ROYGBIV!
Rainbows appear opposite to the Sun. so the Sun is behind you when you see one.

items.name: Volcano
items.text:
A volcano is an opening in the Earth's surface through which hot melted rock erupts.
The melted rock underground is known as magma. Once it erupts, it is known as lava.
Lava can reach temperatures over 1,000 degrees Celsius.
Volcanoes can be active (erupting now), dormant (sleeping), or extinct (finished forever).
Mount Vesuvius in Italy famously buried the city of Pompeii in AD 79.

items.name: Tornado
items.text:
A tornado is a powerful spinning column of air that reaches from a storm cloud to the ground.
It can spin at speeds of over 480 km per hour.
Tornadoes are also known as twisters.
They form during very strong thunderstorms known as supercells.
The USA experiences more tornadoes than any other country. especially in Tornado Alley.

items.name: Thunder and Lightning
items.text:
It happen during powerful thunderstorms.
Lightning is a giant spark of electricity in the sky.
It can occur inside a cloud, between clouds, or between a cloud and the ground.
When lightning heats the air suddenly, the air expands rapidly. this makes the BOOM of thunder!
Lightning moves at about 300,000 km per second. almost the speed of light.

items.name: Glacier
items.text:
A glacier is a very big, slow-moving river of ice that forms over thousands of years.
Glaciers form when more snow falls than melts, compressing into thick ice.
They cover about 10% of Earth's land surface. mostly in Antarctica and Greenland.
Glaciers move very slowly. often just a few centimetres per day.
But as they move, they carve out valleys, fjords, and lakes in the rock below.

items.name: Monsoon
items.text:
The monsoon is a seasonal wind system that brings heavy rainfall to large parts of Asia.
In India, the monsoon arrives around June and lasts until September.
It brings about 80% of India's annual rainfall. essential for farming and water supply.
The word monsoon comes from the Arabic word mausim, meaning season.
Farmers across India wait eagerly for the first rains of the monsoon.

items.name: Earthquake
items.text:
An earthquake is a sudden shaking of the ground caused by movement in the Earth's crust.
The Earth's outer layer is made of giant pieces known as tectonic plates.
When these plates push, pull, or slide against each other. energy is released as shaking.
The point underground where the earthquake starts is known as the focus.
Directly above it on the surface is the epicentre. where shaking is strongest.

items.name: Tsunami
items.text:
A tsunami is a series of enormous ocean waves caused by an underwater earthquake or volcano.
The word tsunami comes from Japanese and means harbour wave.
In the deep ocean, a tsunami wave may only be 30 cm high. nearly invisible.
But as it reaches shallow water near the coast, it grows to tens of metres!
Tsunamis can travel across entire oceans at the speed of a jet plane. 800 km per hour.

items.name: Ocean
items.text:
The ocean covers about 71% of Earth's surface. our planet is mostly water!
There are five oceans: the Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest. it is bigger than all land on Earth combined!
The deepest point is the Mariana Trench in the Pacific. nearly 11 km deep.
The ocean regulates Earth's temperature and weather patterns.

items.name: Desert
items.text:
A desert is a landscape that receives very little rainfall. less than 250 mm per year.
Deserts cover about one-third of Earth's land surface.
The Sahara in Africa is the world's largest hot desert. as big as the USA!
The Antarctic is actually the world's largest desert. it is cold and extremely dry.
Despite being harsh, deserts are full of life. adapted animals and plants live there.

items.name: Forest
items.text:
A forest is a large area of land covered with trees and other plants.
Forests cover about 31% of Earth's land surface.
The Amazon Rainforest in South America is the world's largest tropical forest.
Forests are sometimes known as the lungs of the Earth. they absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen.
Millions of species of animals, plants, fungi, and insects live in forests.

items.name: Waterfall
items.text:
A waterfall is formed when a river flows over a sudden drop in the landscape.
The water plunges down, creating a spectacular sight and thundering sound.
Angel Falls in Venezuela is the world's highest waterfall. nearly 1 km tall!
Niagara Falls on the US-Canada border is the most well known and enormously powerful.
Jog Falls in Karnataka is one of India's most beautiful waterfalls.

items.name: Cave
items.text:
A cave is a natural underground hollow in rock, usually formed by water or lava.
Over thousands of years, water dissolves limestone rock, creating tunnels and chambers.
The longest cave system in the world is Mammoth Cave in the USA. over 660 km of passages!
Caves have unique ecosystems. animals that live only in caves are known as troglobites.
Blind fish, pale spiders, and bats are common cave dwellers.

items.name: Aurora
items.text:
The Aurora Borealis, or Northern Lights, is one of nature's most breathtaking displays.
Colourful ribbons of green, red, blue, and purple dance across the polar night sky.
They occur when charged particles from the Sun strike gases in Earth's upper atmosphere.
Green light is made by oxygen at lower altitudes; red by oxygen at higher altitudes.
Blue and purple come from nitrogen gas being energised.

items.name: Tirupati Balaji
items.text:
It is one of the most visited temples in the entire world.
It is found on the Tirumala hills in Andhra Pradesh, India.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Venkateswara, a form of Lord Vishnu.
Every day, over 50,000 to 1,00,000 pilgrims come to visit.
Devotees offer their hair to the Lord as a sign of devotion.

items.name: Kashi Vishwanath
items.text:
It is one of the holiest temples in Hinduism.
It is found in Varanasi (also known as Kashi), Uttar Pradesh, India.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva. Vishwanath means Lord of the Universe.
The sacred Ganga river flows right beside this very old temple.
Hindus believe that dying in Kashi brings liberation (moksha) from the cycle of rebirth.

items.name: Badrinath Temple
items.text:
It is one of the four sacred dhams (holy sites) of Hinduism.
It is found high in the Himalayan mountains of Uttarakhand, India.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Vishnu in his form as Badrinarayan.
It sits at an altitude of about 3,133 metres above sea level.
Badrinath is one of the Char Dham temples. pilgrims try to visit all four in their lifetime.

items.name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
items.text:
It is a beautiful temple in Madurai, Tamil Nadu.
It is dedicated to Goddess Meenakshi (Parvati) and Lord Sundareswara (Shiva).
The temple complex has 14 grand towers known as gopurams, covered with colourful sculptures.
The tallest gopuram stands about 52 metres high!
Over 30,000 people visit this temple every single day.

items.name: Somnath Temple
items.text:
It is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas. the holiest shrines of Lord Shiva.
It is found on the western coast of Gujarat, facing the Arabian Sea.
Somnath has been attacked and destroyed multiple times throughout history. and rebuilt each time.
The current temple was rebuilt in 1951 after Indian independence, by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel.
Standing by the sea, Somnath is a symbol of resilience and Hindu faith.

items.name: Jagannath Temple Puri
items.text:
Jagannath Temple in Puri, Odisha, is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites.
It is dedicated to Lord Jagannath. a form of Lord Vishnu.
The temple's towering spire rises 65 metres high and can be seen from far away.
The well known Rath Yatra (Chariot Festival) draws millions of devotees every year.
Huge wooden chariots carrying the deities are pulled through the streets by devotees.

items.name: Kedarnath Temple
items.text:
It is one of the most sacred and dramatic temples in India.
It is found at an altitude of 3,583 metres in the Himalayas of Uttarakhand.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas.
Kedarnath is surrounded by beautiful snow-capped peaks on three sides.
The temple is open only from May to November. in winter it is buried under snow.

items.name: Rameshwaram Temple
items.text:
It is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites of Hinduism.
It is found on Pamban Island in Tamil Nadu, connected to the mainland by a bridge.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva. Lord Rama himself is said to have worshipped here.
After defeating Ravana, Rama installed the Shivalingam here in gratitude.
The temple has the longest corridor of any temple in India. over 1.2 km long!

items.name: Golden Temple Amritsar
items.text:
The Golden Temple, or Harmandir Sahib, is the holiest shrine in Sikhism.
It is found in Amritsar, Punjab, India.
The temple is covered in real gold. given by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in the 19th century.
It sits in the middle of a sacred pool known as the Amrit Sarovar. the Pool of Nectar.
Pilgrims bathe in its sacred waters before entering the temple.

items.name: Brihadeeswarar Temple
items.text:
It is one of the greatest temples ever built in India.
It is found in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, and was built by the great Chola king Raja Raja I.
It was completed in 1010 CE. over 1,000 years ago!
The main tower (vimana) rises to 66 metres. one of the tallest in India.
The entire temple was built without modern machinery. only human skill and determination.

items.name: Vaishno Devi Temple
items.text:
It is one of the most visited pilgrimage sites in India.
It is found in the Trikuta Mountains of Jammu and Kashmir.
The temple is dedicated to Goddess Vaishno Devi. a form of the divine mother.
Pilgrims trek about 14 km through beautiful mountain terrain to reach the shrine.
The shrine is inside a natural cave. giving it a deeply mystical atmosphere.

items.name: Lotus Temple Delhi
items.text:
The Lotus Temple in New Delhi is one of the most beautiful modern temples in the world.
It is a Bahai House of Worship. open to people of all religions and backgrounds.
The building is shaped like a giant white lotus flower with 27 petals made of marble.
It was completed in 1986 and has won many architectural awards worldwide.
The Lotus Temple has no idols or religious images inside. only peace and silence.

items.name: Akshardham Temple
items.text:
It in New Delhi is one of the most spectacular temples in the modern world.
It was built by the BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha and inaugurated in 2005.
The main monument is made of pink sandstone and white marble with no steel or concrete used!
Over 3,000 tonnes of stone were carved by skilled artisans from across India.
The monument features 234 ornate pillars, 9 domes, and over 20,000 statues of saints and deities.

items.name: Dwarka Temple
items.text:
Dwarka Temple, or Dwarkadhish Temple, is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites.
It is found in Dwarka, Gujarat, on the western coast of India.
The temple is dedicated to Lord Krishna. Dwarka was his kingdom in very old times.
According to legend, Lord Krishna founded and ruled the city of Dwarka.
After Krishna's departure from the Earth, the original city was said to have submerged in the sea.

items.name: Lord Ganesha
items.text:
It is one of the most beloved gods in Hinduism.
He has the head of an elephant and a kind, chubby human body.
Ganesha is the son of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati.
He is known as Vighnaharta. the remover of obstacles.
Before starting any new work or journey, Hindus pray to Ganesha first.

items.name: Goddess Lakshmi
items.text:
It is the Hindu goddess of wealth, fortune, and beauty.
She is the beloved consort of Lord Vishnu.
Lakshmi is shown seated on a lotus flower, with gold coins flowing from her hands.
She has four hands representing dharma, kama, artha, and moksha.
Her machine is an owl. representing wisdom in the darkness.

items.name: Lord Krishna
items.text:
It is one of the most beloved avatars of Lord Vishnu.
He was born in Mathura and grew up in the village of Vrindavan.
Krishna is well known for his mischievous childhood. stealing butter and playing the flute.
He is known as Makhan Chor (butter thief) among his loving devotees.
Krishna is also a great warrior and wise teacher.

items.name: Lord Hanuman
items.text:
It is one of the greatest devotees of Lord Rama.
He is known for his immense strength, courage, and devotion.
Hanuman is the son of Vayu. the god of wind.
He could fly through the skies and change his size from tiny to enormous.
In the Ramayana, Hanuman crossed the ocean to Lanka to find Sita.

items.name: Lord Shiva
items.text:
It is one of the most powerful gods in Hinduism. part of the holy Trinity.
He is the destroyer and transformer in the cycle of creation, preservation, and destruction.
Shiva lives on Mount Kailash in the Himalayas, deep in meditation.
He wears a crescent moon on his head and the holy Ganga flows from his matted hair.
The third eye on his forehead can open to release tremendous energy.

items.name: Goddess Durga
items.text:
It is the fierce and powerful mother goddess of Hinduism.
She was created by the combined energy of all the gods to defeat the demon Mahishasura.
Durga has eight or ten arms, each holding a divine weapon.
She rides a lion or tiger. symbolising power, courage, and justice.
Durga defeated the demon Mahishasura after nine days of battle. celebrated as Navratri.

items.name: Lord Brahma
items.text:
It is the creator god in Hinduism. part of the divine Trinity (Trimurti).
He created the universe, all living beings, and the four Vedas.
Brahma has four heads, each facing a different direction. symbolising his all-knowing nature.
He holds a water pot, prayer beads, the Vedas, and a lotus in his four hands.
Brahma's machine is Hamsa. the divine swan, a symbol of wisdom.

items.name: Goddess Saraswati
items.text:
It is the Hindu goddess of knowledge, learning, arts, and music.
She is the consort of Lord Brahma and is associated with pure wisdom.
Saraswati is shown wearing pure white. symbolising the purity of knowledge.
She holds a veena (string instrument), a book, prayer beads, and a lotus.
Her machine is a white swan or a peacock.

items.name: Lord Vishnu
items.text:
It is the preserver in the Hindu Trinity. the one who maintains balance in the universe.
He is known as the protector of dharma. righteous order.
Vishnu has four arms holding a conch, discus (Sudarshana Chakra), mace, and lotus.
He rests on the great serpent Adi Shesha, floating on the cosmic ocean.
His machine is Garuda. the mighty eagle king.

items.name: Lord Murugan
items.text:
It is one of the most beloved gods in South India, especially in Tamil Nadu.
He is the son of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati and the younger brother of Lord Ganesha.
Murugan is the god of war, victory, wisdom, and beauty.
He carries a vel (divine spear). symbol of knowledge that cuts through ignorance.
His machine is a peacock. symbol of grace, beauty, and the destruction of ego.

items.name: Goddess Kali
items.text:
It is one of the most powerful and fearsome forms of the divine mother.
She represents the destroyer of evil and the liberator of souls.
Kali is shown with dark blue skin, a garland of skulls, and a sword in her hand.
Her fierce form is not frightening to devotees. it is deeply reassuring.
Kali destroys all negativity. fear, ego, anger, and evil. to keep safe her kids.

items.name: Lord Indra
items.text:
It is the king of the gods (Devas) in Hindu mythology.
He is the god of thunder, lightning, storms, and rain.
Indra wields the Vajra. a divine thunderbolt weapon. as his signature weapon.
His machine is Airavata. a beautiful white elephant.
Indra lives in Swarga (heaven) and rules over all the heavenly realms.

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text:
It is the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu and the hero of the Ramayana.
He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya.
Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam. the ideal man who always follows dharma.
He was a perfect son, husband, brother, and king.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.

items.name: Goddess Parvati
items.text:
It is the divine mother in Hinduism and the consort of Lord Shiva.
She is the goddess of love, fertility, devotion, and divine strength.
Parvati is the gentle, beautiful form of the supreme mother goddess.
She is also known as Uma, Gauri, and Annapurna in different traditions.
Parvati performed intense tapas (meditation and austerity) to win the love of Lord Shiva.

items.name: Diwali
items.text:
It is the most popular festival in India and is known as the Festival of Lights.
It is celebrated every year in October or November.
People light thousands of oil lamps and candles to fill their homes with light.
Fireworks light up the night sky with beautiful colours.
It celebrates the return of Lord Rama to Ayodhya after 14 years of exile.

items.name: Holi
items.text:
It is the festival of colours, celebrated in spring across India.
People throw bright coloured powders and water at each other.
Everyone is covered in pink, red, green, yellow, and blue by the end!
It celebrates the victory of good over evil. the story of Prahlad and Holika.
On the night before Holi, bonfires are lit. this is known as Holika Dahan.

items.name: Navratri
items.text:
It means nine nights and is a festival celebrated for nine days.
It is dedicated to Goddess Durga and her nine forms.
Each day, a different form of Durga is worshipped with different colours and prayers.
Garba and Dandiya are traditional dances performed during Navratri.
Women and girls wear beautiful colourful dresses and dance in circles.

items.name: Raksha Bandhan
items.text:
It is a beautiful festival celebrating the bond between brothers and sisters.
The word means the bond of protection.
On this day, a sister ties a colourful thread known as a rakhi on her brother's wrist.
The rakhi is a symbol of her love and prayer for his long, happy life.
In return, the brother promises to always keep safe his sister and gives her a gift.

items.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
items.text:
It is one of the most joyous festivals in India.
It celebrates the birthday of Lord Ganesha. the elephant-headed god of wisdom.
Beautifully decorated clay idols of Ganesha are installed in homes and public spaces.
For ten days, devotees offer prayers, sweets (especially modak), and flowers.
Mumbai's Ganesh Chaturthi celebrations are among the grandest in the world.

items.name: Pongal
items.text:
It is a harvest festival celebrated in Tamil Nadu in January.
The word Pongal means to boil or overflow. referring to the boiling of the harvest rice.
The festival lasts for four days. Bhogi, Thai Pongal, Mattu Pongal, and Kaanum Pongal.
Farmers thank the Sun, rain, and cattle for giving them a good harvest.
On the main day, fresh rice is cooked in a clay pot with jaggery, milk, and cashews.

items.name: Onam
items.text:
It is the harvest festival of Kerala and one of the most celebrated festivals in South India.
It falls in the Malayalam month of Chingam (August to September).
It celebrates the return of the mythical King Mahabali to visit his beloved people.
The most well known activity of Onam is the Pookalam. a beautiful flower carpet.
Vallamkali, the thrilling snake boat race on the backwaters, is watched by thousands.

items.name: Ugadi
items.text:
It is the New Year festival celebrated in Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Karnataka.
The word Ugadi means the start of a new age in Sanskrit.
It falls on the first day of the Chaitra month. usually in March or April.
It marks the beginning of spring, new crops, and new beginnings.
The Panchanga (almanac) is read on this day to know the year's predictions.

items.name: Janmashtami
items.text:
It is the joyous celebration of the birth of Lord Krishna.
Krishna was born at midnight. so celebrations reach their peak at the stroke of twelve!
Temples are beautifully decorated and filled with devotional songs and dances.
The Dahi Handi is one of the most exciting Janmashtami traditions.
Young men form human pyramids to break a pot of curd hung high up. just like Krishna used to steal butter!

items.name: Maha Shivaratri
items.text:
It is one of the most important festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva.
The name means the Great Night of Shiva. celebrated on the 14th night of the dark moon in Phalguna.
Devotees fast throughout the day and night and stay awake in prayer and devotion.
The Shivalingam is bathed with milk, honey, curd, and water. known as Abhishekam.
Bilva leaves and white flowers are offered to Lord Shiva. deeply sacred to him.

items.name: Maha Shivaratri
items.text:
It is one of the most important festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva.
The name means the Great Night of Shiva. celebrated on the 14th night of the dark moon in Phalguna.
Devotees fast throughout the day and night and stay awake in prayer and devotion.
The Shivalingam is bathed with milk, honey, curd, and water. known as Abhishekam.
Bilva leaves and white flowers are offered to Lord Shiva. deeply sacred to him.

items.name: Christmas
items.text:
It is celebrated on 25th December every year to mark the birth of Jesus Christ.
Christians believe Jesus was the Son of God, born to bring love and salvation to the world.
It is celebrated with joy, lights, decorations, and the exchange of gifts.
The Christmas tree. decorated with lights and ornaments. is a beloved tradition worldwide.
Santa Claus is a beloved figure who is said to deliver gifts to kids on Christmas Eve.

items.name: Baisakhi
items.text:
It is a harvest festival celebrated mainly in Punjab on April 13 or 14 every year.
It marks the beginning of the Sikh New Year and the harvest of the Rabi crop.
On this day in 1699, Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa. the community of baptised Sikhs.
It is therefore one of the most sacred days in Sikhism.
Devotees visit the Gurdwara for prayers, hymns, and langar (community meal).

items.name: Gudi Padwa
items.text:
It is the New Year festival celebrated in Maharashtra, Goa, and parts of Karnataka.
It falls on the first day of the Hindu month of Chaitra. usually March or April.
A Gudi is a bright cloth tied to a bamboo pole, topped with a copper pot and neem leaves.
It is hoisted outside homes as a symbol of victory, prosperity, and the arrival of the New Year.
According to tradition, Lord Brahma created the universe on this day.

items.name: Matsya Avatar
items.text:
Matsya is the first avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant fish.
The word Matsya means fish in Sanskrit.
In this form, Vishnu saved the first man, Manu, from a great flood.
He also rescued the sacred Vedas (very old scriptures) that were stolen by a demon.
Matsya warned Manu to build a great boat and put all living creatures in it.

items.name: Kurma Avatar
items.text:
Kurma is the second avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant tortoise.
Kurma means tortoise in Sanskrit.
The gods and demons once worked together to churn the cosmic ocean to get Amrita. the nectar of immortality.
They used Mount Mandara as a churning rod and the great serpent Vasuki as a rope.
But the mountain began to sink! Vishnu became Kurma and dived to the ocean bed.

items.name: Varaha Avatar
items.text:
Varaha is the third avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a mighty boar.
The demon Hiranyaksha had stolen the Earth (Bhudevi) and hidden her in the cosmic ocean.
Vishnu took the form of a very big boar and dived into the primordial waters.
After a fierce battle with the demon, Varaha lifted the Earth on his tusks.
He brought her back to her rightful place in the universe.

items.name: Narasimha Avatar
items.text:
Narasimha is the fourth avatar of Lord Vishnu. half man, half lion.
Nara means man and Simha means lion in Sanskrit.
The demon king Hiranyakashipu had a boon that he could not be killed by man or animal.
His own son Prahlad was a devoted follower of Lord Vishnu.
Hiranyakashipu tried to kill Prahlad many times, but Vishnu always protected him.

items.name: Vamana Avatar
items.text:
Vamana is the fifth avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a small dwarf Brahmin.
The demon king Bali was very powerful and had conquered the three worlds.
Bali was generous. he never refused anyone who came to him for a gift.
Vishnu took the form of a tiny Brahmin boy and went to Bali's court.
He asked for just three steps of land. as much as his small feet could cover.

items.name: Parashurama Avatar
items.text:
Parashurama is the sixth avatar of Lord Vishnu. a fierce warrior-sage.
The word Parashu means axe. Parashurama means Rama with an axe.
He was born as a Brahmin but was a mighty warrior by the will of Vishnu.
Parashurama lived in an age when corrupt and arrogant kings were causing great suffering.
He is said to have rid the Earth of evil rulers twenty-one times.

items.name: Rama Avatar
items.text:
Rama is the seventh and most celebrated avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He is the hero of the Ramayana. one of India's greatest epics.
Rama was born as the prince of Ayodhya, son of King Dasharatha.
He is known as Maryada Purushottam. the ideal man who always follows dharma.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.

items.name: Krishna Avatar
items.text:
Krishna is the eighth and most beloved avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He appeared in an age of great war and confusion to restore dharma.
Krishna was born in a prison to save the world from the evil king Kansa.
He was secretly sent to Vrindavan where he grew up as a playful cowherd boy.
As a child Krishna killed many demons. showing divine power in a playful, loving form.

items.name: Buddha Avatar
items.text:
The Buddha is considered the ninth avatar of Lord Vishnu in some Hindu traditions.
Siddhartha Gautama was born as a prince in Lumbini (modern-day Nepal) around 563 BCE.
He left his palace after seeing old age, sickness, and death for the first time.
After years of searching, he attained enlightenment while meditating under a Bodhi tree.
He became the Buddha. the Awakened One.

items.name: Kalki Avatar
items.text:
Kalki is the tenth and final avatar of Lord Vishnu. but he has not yet appeared!
Hindu scriptures say Kalki will come at the end of the Kali Yuga. the current age of darkness.
He will ride a white horse, carry a blazing sword, and destroy all evil from the world.
After Kalki's arrival, a new golden age. the Satya Yuga. will begin.
The name Kalki means eternity, or one who destroys darkness.

items.name: The Bhagavad Gita
items.text:
It is one of the most important scriptures in Hinduism.
It is part of the great epic Mahabharata.
The Gita is a conversation between Prince Arjuna and his charioteer Lord Krishna.
The conversation takes place on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, just before a great war.
Arjuna was confused and sad. he did not want to fight his own family.

items.name: Karma Yoga
items.text:
It is one of the paths taught in the Bhagavad Gita.
Karma means action or deed.
Krishna teaches that every action we do has a consequence. good actions bring good results.
The key teaching is: do your duty well, without worrying about reward or result.
This is known as Nishkama Karma. selfless action.

items.name: Arjuna's Dilemma
items.text:
At the start of the Bhagavad Gita, Prince Arjuna faces a terrible problem.
He must fight in a war. but the enemies are his own cousins, uncles, and teachers.
Arjuna's hands tremble and he puts down his bow.
He tells Krishna: I cannot fight. it would be wrong to kill my own family.
Arjuna was a great warrior, but now he was confused and full of grief.

items.name: Gyaana Yoga
items.text:
It is the path of knowledge. one of the four paths in the Bhagavad Gita.
It means seeking deep understanding of the self and the universe.
Krishna teaches that the highest knowledge is knowing the soul (Atman) is part of God (Brahman).
We are not just the body. our true self is the eternal soul inside.
By studying, thinking deeply, and meditating, a person walks the path of Gyaana Yoga.

items.name: Bhakti Yoga
items.text:
It is the path of devotion. loving God with all your heart.
In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says Bhakti Yoga is the easiest and most direct path to God.
Bhakti means deep, pure love for the divine.
A devotee on this path prays, sings hymns, and thinks of God constantly.
Mirabai, Tukaram, and Kabir are well known saints who walked the path of Bhakti.

items.name: The Eternal Soul
items.text:
One of the most important teachings in the Bhagavad Gita is about the soul.
Krishna tells Arjuna: the soul (Atman) can never be killed.
Weapons cannot cut it, fire cannot burn it, water cannot wet it, and wind cannot dry it.
The soul simply moves from one body to another. like changing old clothes for new ones.
This is the basis of the Hindu belief in reincarnation. rebirth after death.

items.name: Nishkama Karma
items.text:
It is one of the most powerful teachings of the Bhagavad Gita.
Nishkama means desireless. doing something without expecting a reward.
Krishna teaches: You have a right to perform your actions, but never to the fruits of those actions.
This means: do your best work, but do not be attached to whether you succeed or fail.
A farmer who plants seeds with love but accepts whatever harvest comes is practising Nishkama Karma.

items.name: Raja Yoga
items.text:
It is the path of meditation and mental discipline in the Bhagavad Gita.
Raja means king. and Raja Yoga makes you the master (king) of your own mind.
Krishna teaches that the mind is our greatest friend. and also our greatest enemy.
A disciplined mind brings peace and clarity. An undisciplined mind brings suffering.
It involves sitting quietly, focusing the mind, and going inward.

items.name: Dharma
items.text:
It is one of the central concepts in the Bhagavad Gita and in all of Hindu thought.
The word dharma has many meanings. duty, righteousness, law, and the right way of living.
Krishna tells Arjuna: it is better to follow your own dharma imperfectly than another's dharma perfectly.
Every person has their own dharma. the right duty for their role in life.
A student's dharma is to study with sincerity and respect.

items.name: The Battlefield Lesson
items.text:
The Bhagavad Gita begins on a battlefield. and this setting has a deep meaning.
Life itself is a kind of battlefield where we face difficult choices every day.
Just as Arjuna faced his own relatives as enemies, we sometimes face conflicts with those we love.
Krishna teaches that the right response is not to run away but to stand firm with wisdom.
The battlefield represents the struggle between our higher self and our lower desires.

items.name: The Pandavas
items.text:
It is the five heroic brothers at the heart of the Mahabharata.
Their names are: Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva.
They were the sons of King Pandu of Hastinapura.
Yudhishthira was the eldest and known for his honesty and righteousness.
Bhima was the strongest of all. he could wrestle demons and elephants!

items.name: The Kauravas
items.text:
It is the one hundred sons of King Dhritarashtra of Hastinapura.
Duryodhana was the eldest and most powerful of the Kauravas.
It were the rivals and cousins of the Pandavas.
Duryodhana was proud, jealous, and deeply resentful of the Pandavas' virtues.
He refused to give even five villages to the Pandavas. leading to the great war.

items.name: Battle of Kurukshetra
items.text:
The Battle of Kurukshetra is the great war at the centre of the Mahabharata.
It was fought between the Pandavas and the Kauravas. two branches of the same family.
The battle lasted for 18 days on the field of Kurukshetra in Haryana, India.
Lord Krishna served as the charioteer of Arjuna and guided him with wisdom.
It was before this battle that Krishna shared the wisdom of the Bhagavad Gita.

items.name: Draupadi
items.text:
It is one of the most powerful women in the Mahabharata.
She was born from a sacred fire during a yagna performed by her father, King Drupada.
It was the wife of all five Pandava brothers.
She was known for her beauty, intelligence, fierce spirit, and deep devotion to Krishna.
In one of the darkest moments of the story, she was publicly humiliated by the Kauravas.

items.name: Karna
items.text:
It is one of the most tragic and admired heroes of the Mahabharata.
He was actually the eldest son of Kunti. making him the Pandavas' own brother!
But Kunti gave him up as a baby, and he was raised by a charioteer named Adhiratha.
Because of his humble birth, Karna was often mocked despite his great talent.
He was the greatest rival of Arjuna in archery. their skill was equal.

items.name: Bhishma
items.text:
It is one of the greatest and most tragic figures of the Mahabharata.
His original name was Devavrata. son of King Shantanu and the goddess Ganga.
He is known as Bhishma because of his terrible vow: he swore never to marry or become king.
This was to allow his father to marry Satyavati, a fisherman's daughter.
It had the boon of choosing his own time of death. he could not die without his will.

items.name: Abhimanyu
items.text:
It is one of the most heroic young warriors in the Mahabharata.
He was the son of the great archer Arjuna and Subhadra, Krishna's sister.
It learned the art of entering the Chakravyuha (a circular military formation) while in the womb!
He heard his father Arjuna explaining it to his mother. but Subhadra fell asleep before Arjuna explained how to exit.
So Abhimanyu knew how to enter the Chakravyuha but not how to come out.

items.name: Vidura
items.text:
It is one of the wisest and most virtuous characters of the Mahabharata.
He was the half-brother of Dhritarashtra and Pandu. born of a servant woman.
Despite his lower birth, Vidura became the chief minister of Hastinapura.
He was known as the embodiment of dharma. always speaking the truth.
It repeatedly warned King Dhritarashtra against the wicked schemes of Duryodhana.

items.name: Shakuni
items.text:
It was the maternal uncle of the Kauravas and one of the most cunning characters in the Mahabharata.
He was the prince of Gandhara (present-day Afghanistan and Pakistan).
It carried a deep grudge against the Kuru dynasty for the suffering of his family.
He used his cunning and mastery of dice to manipulate events against the Pandavas.
It was Shakuni who rigged the well known game of dice where Yudhishthira lost everything. including Draupadi.

items.name: Drona
items.text:
Drona, also known as Dronacharya, was the greatest teacher of warfare in the Mahabharata.
He taught both the Pandavas and Kauravas the art of archery and military strategy.
Drona's favourite student was Arjuna. he believed Arjuna would become the greatest archer in the world.
The story of Ekalavya is one of the most well known and moving stories involving Drona.
Ekalavya, a tribal boy, taught himself archery by worshipping a clay statue of Drona.

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text:
It is the hero of the Ramayana and the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya.
Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam. the ideal man who always followed dharma.
He was a perfect son, husband, brother, and king.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.

items.name: Sita
items.text:
It is the beloved wife of Lord Rama and the heroine of the Ramayana.
She was found as a baby in a furrow of the earth by King Janaka. hence known as Janaki.
It is worshipped as an ideal of purity, love, courage, and devotion.
She chose to follow Rama into the forest during his 14 years of exile out of pure love.
When Ravana kidnapped her, Sita never gave up hope. she kept her faith in Rama.

items.name: Ravana
items.text:
It was the powerful king of Lanka and the main villain of the Ramayana.
He had ten heads and twenty arms, symbolising his vast knowledge and power.
It was actually a great scholar. he knew the Vedas and played the veena beautifully.
He was a devoted worshipper of Lord Shiva and had received many powerful boons.
But Ravana's pride and ego led him to kidnap Sita. and this became his downfall.

items.name: Hanuman's Leap to Lanka
items.text:
One of the greatest moments in the Ramayana is when Hanuman leaps across the ocean to Lanka.
Sita had been kidnapped by Ravana and Rama needed to know where she was.
Hanuman volunteered to search for Sita.
He climbed a mountain, took a deep breath, and leaped across the vast ocean!
On the way, he faced sea monsters and storms. but nothing stopped him.

items.name: Lakshmana
items.text:
It is the devoted younger brother and constant companion of Lord Rama.
When Rama was sent into exile, Lakshmana refused to stay behind.
He chose to leave the comfort of the palace and follow Rama into the forest out of pure love.
It built huts, gathered food, and stood guard every night to keep safe Rama and Sita.
He barely slept during the entire 14 years of exile. his protectiveness was fierce.

items.name: Vibhishana
items.text:
It was the younger brother of Ravana. but unlike his brother, he was righteous and good.
He repeatedly advised Ravana to return Sita and avoid war with Rama.
But Ravana, blinded by pride, refused to listen.
It finally left Lanka and surrendered to Rama. asking for his protection.
Some advisors said Rama should not trust an enemy's brother.

items.name: Ayodhya
items.text:
It is the sacred birthplace of Lord Rama and one of the holiest cities in Hinduism.
The name Ayodhya means a city that cannot be conquered. a city of peace.
It is found on the banks of the Sarayu river in Uttar Pradesh, India.
It is one of the seven most sacred pilgrimage cities in Hinduism.
The beautiful Ram Mandir was inaugurated in 2024 on the site believed to be Rama's birthplace.

items.name: Jatayu
items.text:
It is one of the most moving and heroic characters in the Ramayana.
He was a mighty eagle. the king of vultures. and an old friend of King Dasharatha.
When Ravana was flying back to Lanka with the kidnapped Sita, Jatayu spotted them.
Despite being old and knowing he faced the most powerful demon king, Jatayu attacked Ravana without hesitation.
He fought bravely in the skies to stop Ravana and save Sita.

items.name: Sugriva
items.text:
It is the monkey king who became one of Rama's most important allies in the Ramayana.
He was the younger brother of the mighty Vali, who had unfairly driven him away from his kingdom.
It and Rama made a pact. Rama would help him defeat Vali, and Sugriva would help find Sita.
After Vali's defeat, Sugriva became the king of Kishkindha.
He organised his vast monkey army to search for Sita across the entire world.

items.name: The Ram Setu
items.text:
The Ram Setu, or Adam's Bridge, is a chain of limestone shoals connecting India and Sri Lanka.
According to the Ramayana, it was built by Rama's army of monkeys to cross over to Lanka.
The bridge is known as Nala Setu after the engineer monkey Nala, son of Vishwakarma, who designed it.
The monkeys brought huge boulders and stones that floated because the name of Rama was written on them.
The devotion of Rama's army made even stones float. a miracle of faith and love.

items.name: Valmiki
items.text:
It is one of the greatest sages in Hindu tradition.
He is known as the Adi Kavi. the first poet in Sanskrit literature.
It composed the Ramayana. one of the longest and most beloved epics in the world.
Interestingly, Valmiki was once a highway robber known as Ratnakar.
A meeting with the sage Narada changed his life. he meditated deeply and was reborn as a great saint.

items.name: Adi Shankaracharya
items.text:
It was one of the greatest philosophers and saints of India.
He was born in Kaladi, Kerala, around 788 CE.
By the age of 8, he had mastered all the Vedas and very old scriptures!
Shankaracharya walked the entire length of India on foot, spreading the message of Advaita Vedanta.
Advaita means non-duality. the teaching that God, the world, and the self are ultimately one.

items.name: Mirabai
items.text:
It was a great saint-poetess from Rajasthan who lived in the 1500s.
From childhood, she was completely devoted to Lord Krishna. she saw him as her true husband.
She was a princess who married a Rajput king, but her heart always belonged to Krishna.
Her in-laws tried to stop her devotion and even tried to harm her. but she was miraculously protected.
It composed hundreds of beautiful bhajans (devotional songs) to Krishna.

items.name: Kabir
items.text:
It was a great poet-saint of the 15th century whose teachings touched both Hindus and Muslims.
He was born in Varanasi and raised by a Muslim weaver family.
It believed that God is one. whether you call him Rama or Allah.
His poems (dohas) were simple, direct, and full of wisdom.
He criticised blind rituals, caste discrimination, and religious hypocrisy fearlessly.

items.name: Swami Vivekananda
items.text:
It was one of the greatest spiritual leaders and thinkers of modern India.
He was born Narendranath Datta in Kolkata in 1863.
He became the most well known disciple of the great mystic Sri Ramakrishna Paramahamsa.
Vivekananda introduced Hinduism to the Western world at the Parliament of World Religions in Chicago in 1893.
His speech beginning Sisters and Brothers of America electrified the audience!

items.name: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa
items.text:
It was one of the greatest mystics and saints of 19th-century India.
He was born in 1836 in a village in West Bengal.
From childhood he was deeply drawn to the divine. he would go into trances of ecstasy.
He served as a priest at the Dakshineswar Kali Temple near Kolkata.
Ramakrishna had profound mystical experiences with Goddess Kali. he saw her as a living reality.

items.name: Tukaram
items.text:
It was a great Bhakti saint from Maharashtra who lived in the 17th century.
He was a devotee of Lord Vitthal (Vishnu) at Pandharpur.
It was a simple farmer who experienced great personal tragedy. losing his first wife and son in a famine.
Through his grief, he turned completely to God and began composing abhangas. devotional poems.
His abhangas are written in simple Marathi and speak of love, surrender, and devotion to Vitthal.

items.name: Ramanujacharya
items.text:
It was a great Vaishnava saint and philosopher of the 11th to 12th century.
He was born in Sriperumbudur near Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
Ramanuja taught Vishishtadvaita. the philosophy that the soul and God are distinct but always connected.
He believed deeply in devotion (bhakti) to Lord Vishnu as the path to liberation.
Ramanuja opened the doors of temples to people of all backgrounds. a revolutionary act of equality.

items.name: Narada Muni
items.text:
It is one of the most fascinating and important sages in Hindu mythology.
He is the son of Lord Brahma and moves freely between the three worlds. earth, heaven, and the underworld.
Narada is always depicted with a veena (string instrument), chanting the name of Narayana (Vishnu).
He is the divine messenger. carrying news between gods, sages, and humans.
Narada is the author of the Narada Bhakti Sutras. a beautiful text on the nature of devotion.

items.name: Vyasa
items.text:
Vyasa, also known as Veda Vyasa, is one of the most important sages in all of Hindu tradition.
He is credited with compiling and organising the four Vedas. the oldest scriptures in Hinduism.
It also composed the Mahabharata. the longest epic poem ever written, with over 100,000 verses!
He is the grandfather figure of the Mahabharata. grandfather to both the Pandavas and Kauravas.
It also composed the 18 major Puranas. encyclopaedias of Hindu mythology and wisdom.

comprehension.subject: Honeybees
comprehension.text: Honeybees are amazing little insects that live together in large groups called colonies. A colony can have up to 60,000 bees! Every bee in the colony has a job to do. The queen bee lays all the eggs. Worker bees collect nectar from flowers and bring it back to the hive. They turn the nectar into honey by fanning it with their wings to dry it out. Bees also help flowers grow by carrying pollen from one flower to another — this is called pollination. Without bees, many of the fruits and vegetables we eat would not exist. So the next time you see a bee buzzing around a flower, remember — it is doing very important work!

questions.question: What do worker bees collect from flowers?
How do bees turn nectar into honey?
options: By fanning it with their wings
questions.correct: By fanning it with their wings
